data.title: U.S. stocks seesaw as more corporate earnings lift sentiment amid government shutdown
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages pared their gains on Thursday as investors continued to assess earnings reports while the U.S. government shutdown entered its third week.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last flat in midday trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.3%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was also flat. 

On earnings, Charles Schwab (SCHW) shares rose +1.2% after earnings beat. U.S. Bancorp (USB) also reported earnings growth, with shares +1.1%

“The good news is that earnings are doing the heavy lifting as we enter year 4 of this bull market. Following Friday’s decline, the P/E ratio is only up 1% year-over-year, while earnings are up 11%. This is in line with my expectation at the beginning of the year that after two years of substantial P/E expansion, earnings would become the dominant driver of returns in 2025,” said Jurrien Timmer, director of global macro at Fidelity.

“If history is any guide, we should see a meaningful bounce from the 7% growth estimate. A good bounce seems likely following the sharp uptick in earnings revisions in recent months,” Timmer added.

In other tech news, Salesforce (CRM) +4.3% and Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) announced an expansion of their existing partnership on Thursday to bring Gemini AI models to the former Agentforce 360 Platform.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 2 basis points to 4.02%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) was 2 basis point lower at 3.49%.

On trade, Treasury Secretary Bessent had reportedly said that President Donald Trump "is a go" for a meeting with Xi Jinping and that he was positive about the ongoing discussions with China. 

The U.S. government shutdown is now entering its third week. Prediction market Kalshi now sees the government shutdown lasting more than 39 days.

The Fed balance sheet is due later in the day. 

U.S. stocks ended mixed after a choppy session on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) gaining ground as upbeat earnings reports eclipsed U.S.-China trade tension worries.

data.title: U.S. nuclear weapons workforce faces furloughs if shutdown persists, Energy Secretary warns
data.description:
Energy Secretary Chris Wright warned that thousands of workers responsible for maintaining and modernizing the United States’ nuclear arsenal could soon be furloughed if the federal government shutdown drags on past Friday.

Speaking Thursday on Bloomberg Television, Wright said the National Nuclear Security Administration (NNSA), the Energy Department division overseeing the nation’s nuclear weapons stockpile, will be unable to pay many of its employees and contractors within days.

“Starting tomorrow, Monday at the latest, we are not going to be able to pay those workers,” he said. “If that continues on for long, they may get other jobs. They’re going to stop their efforts to modernize our nuclear weapons, to guarantee the sovereignty of our country, something we shouldn’t mess around with.”

The NNSA, which operates semi-independently within the Energy Department, manages the production and dismantling of nuclear weapons, supplies naval nuclear reactor, and leads responses to radiological and nuclear emergencies. It also supports counterterrorism operations and secures nuclear materials worldwide.

Wright said as many as 100,000 contract workers could be affected, noting that unlike federal employees, they may not qualify for back pay once the shutdown ends. The department expects to stagger the furloughs to preserve essential operations as long as possible.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: U.S. judge poised to approve Rio Tinto's $138M Oyu Tolgoi mine settlement
data.description:
A U.S. judge said he is ready to approve Rio Tinto's (NYSE:RIO) agreement to pay $138.7 million to settle a lawsuit that accused the company of defrauding investors by concealing problems with its $7 billion underground expansion of the Oyu Tolgoi copper and gold mine in Mongolia, Reuters reported Wednesday.

Rio (NYSE:RIO) had reached a preliminary settlement with shareholders of the former Turquoise Hill Resources in June, pending approval from U.S. District Judge Lewis Liman, who said at a hearing on Wednesday that he was ready to approve the deal, but did not sign off yet because he wanted the shareholders' lawyers to let him know what they planned to do with any funds left after initial distributions.

Shareholders led by Pentwater Capital Management accused Rio (RIO) and Turquoise Hill of fraudulently assuring the Oyu Tolgoi mine was "on plan" and "on budget," even as it was falling more than two years behind schedule and running as much as $1.9 billion over budget.

The lawsuit sought damages on behalf of Turquoise Hill shareholders between July 2018 and July 2019, when that company was majority-owned by Rio Tinto (RIO); Turquoise Hill had owned 66% of the Oyu Tolgoi mine, with Mongolia's government owning 34%.

In 2022, Rio Tinto (RIO) bought the 49% of Turquoise Hill it did not already own for $3.3 billion, fully integrating the mine into its copper portfolio.

data.title: Wolfspeed moves higher as Voya Financial discloses stake
data.description:
• Wolfspeed (NYSE:WOLF) shares moved higher by more than 2% on Thursday as asset manager Voya Financial disclosed a stake in the restructured silicon carbide maker.
  • The asset manager owns 18.3% of the Delaware-based Wolfspeed, according to a 13G filing. The 5.3M share stake is passive.
  • Wolfspeed emerged from its Chapter 11 restructuring process last month. As part of the restructuring, the company drastically reduced the number of shares, significantly cut its debt, relocated its headquarters from North Carolina to Delaware and added five new members to its board of directors.

data.title: Synovus outlines 4.5% loan growth target and signals accelerated hiring pace amid Pinnacle merger integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Synovus Financial Corp. (SNV) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Kevin Blair reported "strong third quarter 2025 GAAP earnings per share of $1.33 and adjusted earnings per share of $1.46, up 19% year-over-year." Blair highlighted that "adjusted PPNR growth was also quite healthy, up 5% sequentially and 12% from the third quarter 2024." He attributed earnings growth to "net interest margin expansion, healthy noninterest revenue growth and lower provision for credit losses." Blair emphasized the company's continued hiring momentum, stating, "25 new revenue producers added during the third quarter for a total of 74 year-to-date." He reiterated confidence in performance, even as the pending merger with Pinnacle Financial Partners approaches, adding "we continue to expect our pending merger with Pinnacle to close in first quarter 2026."
  • Blair also noted significant merger integration progress: "The entire leadership team has been finalized and communicated and all headcount-related decisions and employee communication should be complete in the fourth quarter."
  • CFO Andrew Gregory stated, "Excluding merger-related expenses, Synovus generated positive operating leverage in the third quarter. Adjusted revenue increased 9% year-over-year, while adjusted noninterest expense rose 6%." Gregory said, "Net interest margin expansion drove 3% linked quarter and 8% year-over-year net interest income growth." He reported, "Our high-growth verticals, particularly specialty lending, continue to drive loan growth, while institutional commercial real estate lending was also a strong contributor."

Outlook

  • Blair said, "Period-end loan growth for the year is expected to be approximately 4.5% with broad-based growth expected in the fourth quarter." He stated, "We project core deposit growth of approximately 0.5 percentage point for the year."
  • The CEO described a "modestly more asset sensitive" interest rate profile as Synovus prepares for the merger, warning that "the net interest margin should be under some modest pressure in the fourth quarter."
  • Blair projected "adjusted 2025 revenue growth outlook is 6.5%." He added, "We anticipate adjusted noninterest revenue of $515 million to $520 million this year."
  • He also forecasted "adjusted noninterest expense growth should be 2.5% in 2025." Net charge-offs are estimated "between 15 and 20 basis points in the fourth quarter." Blair expects a "CET1 ratio of approximately 11.35% at year-end 2025."

Financial Results

  • Synovus reported "GAAP earnings per share of $1.33 and adjusted earnings per share of $1.46." Adjusted PPNR increased 5% sequentially and 12% year-over-year.
  • Net interest margin rose "4 basis points to 3.41%." Average loans increased 1% on a linked-quarter basis, with period-end loans up 0.5%.
  • Loan production increased 43% year-over-year. Period-end core deposits declined $231 million or 1% sequentially, mainly due to a strategic decline in public funds.
  • Adjusted noninterest revenue was $136 million, up 4% sequentially and 12% year-over-year. Year-over-year, core banking fees grew 11%, wealth revenue 5%, and capital markets income 36%.
  • Adjusted noninterest expense rose 3% sequentially and 6% year-over-year. Merger-related expenses totaled $24 million.
  • Net charge-offs were $15 million or 14 basis points. Nonperforming assets improved to 0.53% of total loans and real estate owned, down from 0.59% in the prior quarter. CET1 ratio was 11.24%.

Q&A

  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: Asked about capital build and share buybacks post-merger. Gregory answered they expect to close with 10.1% headline CET1 and intend to build capital in early quarters post-close, emphasizing "the earnings power of this institution is so strong that before you consider risk-weighted asset growth, we believe we will generate 35 to 40 basis points of common equity Tier 1 each quarter."
  • Mealor also asked about deposit trends. Blair responded, "We produced about $2.6 billion in new deposit production this quarter. It was actually up 18% versus the second quarter."
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Asked about hiring pace and credit trends. Blair described "general excitement" among internal and prospective hires, attributing momentum to the combined company's appeal. On credit, Anne Fortner said, "We did have about $30 million of NPL outflows that were related to our C&I portfolio... we were encouraged by just the diversity of that activity."
  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC: Inquired about feedback since the merger announcement. Blair outlined five reasons for increased conviction in the merger, including "deeper familiarity with both sides leadership teams," key decisions on talent and technology, and "retention and excitement within the key talent."
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: Asked about loan production and paydowns. Gregory emphasized "production this quarter and last quarter in a lot of our key businesses, and it's a real positive trend." Blair addressed hiring competition, stating confidence in the Pinnacle model for team-based recruitment.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on capital deployment, deposit trends, hiring pace, credit quality, and merger integration, showing a slightly positive but cautious tone, especially regarding the sustainability of loan growth and the impact of elevated paydowns.
  • Management maintained a confident and assertive stance, frequently emphasizing momentum, resilience, and merger progress, with Blair stating "each day I grow more convinced and convicted of the extraordinary potential and the power of our new franchise."
  • Compared with the previous quarter, analysts remained focused on loan growth and hiring, but merger integration and synergy realization became more prominent. Management's tone shifted to even greater conviction, especially regarding merger prospects and organizational alignment.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2025 loan growth narrowed from "4% to 6%" in Q2 to "approximately 4.5%" in Q3; core deposit growth expectation was revised from "1% to 3%" to "0.5%".
  • Revenue growth outlook moved from "5% to 7%" to "6.5%".
  • Management's tone became even more confident, focusing on merger integration and the acceleration of hiring. Analysts' questions shifted from a focus on competitive landscape and hiring to a stronger emphasis on integration and capital management post-merger.
  • The company reported the "lowest net charge-offs in almost 3 years" and "highest CET1 levels in Synovus' history."

Risks and Concerns

  • Elevated paydowns and lower CIB utilization cited as headwinds to loan growth, partially offset by strong production.
  • Deposit decline driven by strategic reduction in public funds, with management confident about seasonal rebound.
  • Merger-related nonrecurring expenses of $24 million noted, along with ongoing integration risks.
  • Management highlighted continued focus on credit risk management and merger integration progress as mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Synovus enters the fourth quarter with strong financial momentum, highlighted by robust earnings growth, loan production, and capital levels, while actively advancing its merger with Pinnacle Financial Partners. Management projects continued broad-based growth in loans and revenue, expects a modestly asset-sensitive balance sheet, and signals a disciplined approach to expense and credit management. The organization is accelerating talent acquisition, finalizing integration decisions, and remains confident in achieving strategic and financial goals for the combined entity in 2026 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dianthus to license autoimmune disease candidate in a deal worth up $1B
data.description:
Dianthus Therapeutics (NASDAQ:DNTH) announced a licensing deal worth up to $1B with Nanjing Leads Biolabs on Thursday to obtain exclusive rights to an autoimmune disease therapy developed by the Chinese biotech.

Based on preclinical data, Dianthus (NASDAQ:DNTH) highlighted how the drug candidate, DNTH212, could work against serious autoimmune diseases and underscored its potential as a first-line biologic given its less frequent dosing and subcutaneous self-administration.

Per the deal terms, the company will receive exclusive global rights to develop and commercialize DNTH212 outside China. In return, it will pay Leads Biolabs up to $30M in upfront and near-term milestones, in addition to $8M pending the start of a Phase 1 trial.

The Hong Kong-based biotech is also eligible to receive $962M in total development as well as regulatory and sales-based milestones in addition to tiered royalties based on the drug’s sales outside China.

In conjunction with the announcement, Dianthus (NASDAQ:DNTH), citing preliminary unaudited data, projected that its cash balance as of Sept. 30 stands at roughly $525M, excluding $30M paid to Leads Biolabs for upfront and near-term milestones. The company continues to expect its cash runway to extend into 2028.

data.title: Sentinel Capital sells NSI Industries' HVAC division to Lennox for $550M
data.description:
• Private equity firm Sentinel Capital Partners announced on Thursday the sale of NSI Industries' HVAC division—which includes Duro Dyne and Supco brands—to Lennox International (NYSE:LII) for approximately $550M in cash.
  • With this divestiture, NSI will refocus as a pure-play manufacturer of branded electrical products sold under names like Bridgeport, Polaris, and Tork. 
  • Insulated connectors, fittings, and other high-value-add components with steady, replacement-driven demand will remain the core of NSI's offering.

data.title: BNY Q3 earnings beat on higher net interest income, AUC/A
data.description:
BNY (NYSE:BK) delivered Monday third-quarter earnings that surpassed the Wall Street consensus as the custodial bank's net interest income was buoyed by the reinvestment of maturing investment securities at higher yields and balance sheet growth, partially offset by changes in deposit mix.

Q3 adjusted EPS of $1.91, topping the $1.77 average analyst estimate, slipped from $1.94 in the prior quarter, but climbed from $1.52 a year ago.

Total revenue of $5.08B, vs. $4.98B expected, drifted up from $5.03B in Q2 and $4.65B in Q3 2024.

BK shares edged up 0.3% in late morning trading.

Net interest income rose to $1.24B from $1.20B in Q2 and $1.05B in last year's Q3.

Fee revenue was $3.64B, virtually unchanged from Q2 and up from $3.40B in Q3 2024. The Y/Y increase reflected net new business, higher client activity and market values, and the favorable impact of a weaker U.S. dollar, partially offset by the mix of assets-under-management flows.

Q3 assets under custody/administration of $57.8T gained 4% sequentially and 11% Y/Y.

Average deposits were $299.3B in Q3 vs. $300.3B in Q2. Average loans of $72.7B in Q3 compared with $71.3B in Q2.

data.title: It's a 'dangerous' time to bet against whipping-boy stocks – Goldman Sachs’ Pasquariello
data.description:
It has been particularly dangerous to be short popular shorts, said Tony Pasquariello, head of hedge fund coverage at Goldman Sachs.

Within stocks with significant short interest, local price action has been rising, more than doubling off the April lows.

It has also generated the third-longest six-month rally in the past 30 years, aside from March 2000 and January 2021.

 

 

 

Below are the stocks with some of the highest short interest that are also rated “Buy” by Seeking Alpha’s Quant metrics.

  • uniQure N.V. (QURE) – Short interest: 17.91%; Quant rating: 4.99
  • Viasat (VSAT) – Short interest: 11.59%; Quant rating: 4.98

  • CytomX Therapeutics (CTMX) – Short interest: 19.02%; Quant rating: 4.97
  • Hut 8 Corp. (HUT) – Short interest: 13.71%; Quant rating: 4.97

  • Lumentum Holdings (LITE) – Short interest: 14.43%; Quant rating: 4.94

  • Pagaya Technologies (PGY) – Short interest: 17.25%; Quant rating: 4.92

Seeking Alpha's Quant system consistently outperforms the S&P 500 (SP500), driven by powerful computer processing and SA's special 'Quantamental' analysis. It awards grades to stocks based on collective value, growth, profitability, earnings per share revisions, and price momentum metrics.

data.title: KeyCorp delivers Q3 earnings, revenue beats, helped by strong noninterest income growth
data.description:
KeyCorp (NYSE:KEY) stock slipped 3.6% in Thursday morning trading, even after its Q3 earnings and revenue beat Wall Street consensus estimates. Q3 net interest income ("NII") came in line with the consensus, which could have underwhelmed investors.

The bank now expects full-year NII (taxable equivalent) to rise ~22% from 2024's $3.81B, implying 2205 NII of ~$4.65B vs. Visible Alpha consensus of $4.64B. Net interest margin is projected to be 2.75%-2.80%, compared with the prior guidance of ~2.75%. 

Adjusted noninterest income is projected to rise 5%-6% Y/Y from 2024's $2.65B, up from the previous outlook of +5%. Guidance of adjusted noninterest expense was refined to ~4% increase vs. the prior range of +3%-5%.

Its guidance for 2025 ending loans up ~2% is unchanged from its prior view.

Meanwhile, KeyCorp (NYSE:KEY) expects its 2025 GAAP tax rate to be ~21%, compared with its earlier guidance of ~21%-22%.

Q3 adjusted EPS of $0.41, beating the $0.38 consensus, rose from $0.35 in Q2 and $0.30 in last year's Q3.

Total revenue (taxable equivalent) of $1.90B, topping the average analyst estimate of $1.88B, increased from $1.84B in the prior quarter and $695M in the year-ago period.

"Revenue growth was driven by our clearly defined net interest income tailwinds and adjusted noninterest income growth of 8%, which continues to grow faster than expenses," said Chairman and CEO Chris Gorman.

He added, "Business momentum with clients and prospects continues to build. Client deposits grew 2% quarter-over-quarter, and relationship households continue to grow at an annualized rate of 2%."

Evercore ISI analyst John Pancari wrote, "Overall, (a) solid quarter with trends intact, fee upside – as well as modest beats across NII and expenses... FY25 outlook revised higher on both NII & fees, albeit somewhat anticipated."

Q3 NII ("TE") of $1.19B, matching the Bloomberg consensus, climbed from $1.15B in Q2 and $964M in Q3 2024.

KeyCorp's (NYSE:KEY) noninterest income of $702M vs. $690M in the previous quarter and $649M a year ago.

Provision for credit losses of $107M, vs. the Bloomberg consensus of $106.3M, fell from $138M in Q2 and increased from $95M in last year's Q3.

Trust and investment services income of $150M increased 2.7% Q/Q and 7.1% Y/Y.

Investment banking and debt placement fees were $184M grew 3.4% Q/Q and 7.6% Y/Y.

Average deposits of $150.4B rose 2% Q/Q. Average loans of $106.2B vs. $105.7B in Q2.
